ontology.foodex2_description: Cheese (semi-hard, brined) — closest parent category for halloumi in FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated halloumi code; A00LC (semi-hard cheese) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT does not have a halloumi-specific concept; 226760005 (Cheese substance) is the closest parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of grilled halloumi is approximately 80 g, equivalent to two to three slices served warm.

nutrient_source.title: Halloumi cheese, grilled — USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy / Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for halloumi cheese (NDB No. 01174 and related entries). Values represent the cheese as typically consumed; grilling causes minimal macro change relative to raw weight. Per-100g values used directly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for halloumi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 27 is estimated by analogy with other full-fat semi-hard cheeses (e.g., cheddar, feta) which have GI values in the range 0–30 per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The very low carbohydrate content (2.4 g/100g) means the glycemic load per 80 g serving is approximately 0.5, confirming negligible glycemic impact. gi_category set to low.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (1440 mg per 100 g) due to brine-curing process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit halloumi intake to small portions (≤40 g) and avoid adding additional salt, as a single 80 g serving provides approximately 1152 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.4 g/100g) and low glycemic index (~27)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Halloumi has a negligible glycemic impact due to its very low carbohydrate content and low GI, making it a suitable protein- and fat-rich food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; however, the high saturated fat content warrants attention in the context of overall dietary fat quality.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (16.5 g/100g) and high sodium (1440 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat and sodium content of halloumi are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict portion size to ≤40 g per occasion and ensure overall dietary saturated fat remains within recommended limits (less than 10% of total energy intake).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Halloumi is a semi-hard, brined cheese originating from Cyprus and widely consumed across the Eastern Mediterranean and Middle Eastern culinary traditions. It is traditionally made from a blend of sheep's and goat's milk, though commercial versions may include cow's milk. The cheese is preserved in brine, which gives it a distinctively salty flavour and a firm, springy texture. Its most notable culinary property is its high melting point, which allows it to be grilled, pan-fried, or barbecued without losing its shape, resulting in a golden-brown crust with a soft, squeaky interior. In Singapore, halloumi is served at Middle Eastern and Mediterranean restaurants, typically as a starter or side dish, often accompanied by salads, flatbreads, or dips such as hummus. Nutritionally, halloumi is energy-dense (approximately 321 kcal per 100 g), high in protein (21.4 g/100g), and high in fat (25 g/100g), with saturated fat comprising the majority of the fat content (16.5 g/100g). It is an excellent source of calcium (700 mg/100g) and provides meaningful amounts of vitamin B12, phosphorus, and zinc. The sodium content is very high (1440 mg/100g) due to the brine-curing process, making it a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The carbohydrate content is very low (2.4 g/100g), resulting in a low glycemic index and negligible glycemic load, which is favourable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Halloumi is naturally gluten-free and suitable for vegetarian diets.
common_user_queries: Is halloumi good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, which provides a well-documented halloumi cheese entry. GI is estimated by analogy with closely related full-fat semi-hard cheeses from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, with the estimation basis clearly documented. Ontology codes are real and defensible. All mandatory sections are complete.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — halloumi is an animal-derived dairy product containing no plant fibre. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg per 100 g; this is naturally zero as halloumi is an animal-derived dairy product. Trans fat of 0.5 g/100g represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fats (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid) present in sheep and goat milk products, not industrially produced trans fats. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on full-fat semi-hard cheese GI values from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct halloumi GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The food is served at Sahara Bar & Restaurant (58 Boat Quay, Singapore) per field survey source data. Price SGD 15.26 per serving as recorded in source. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 corresponds to halloumi cheese in the FoodOn ontology. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as closest parent (semi-hard cheese) as no halloumi-specific FoodEx2 code exists in the published hierarchy.
